_meta.authoringNotes: Sapporo criteria (Miyakis et al. J Thromb Haemost 2006, PMID 16420554) and 2023 ACR/EULAR APS classification criteria (Barbhaiya et al. Ann Rheum Dis 2023). TRAPS trial (Pengo et al. Blood 2018) on rivaroxaban vs warfarin. Pregnancy management from RCOG 2017 and Lockshin et al. Arthritis Rheum 2012. Catastrophic APS registry data (Cervera et al. Autoimmun Rev 2014). Hydroxychloroquine evidence from Erkan et al. Ann Rheum Dis 2002.

heroOverview: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS), also called Hughes syndrome, is an autoimmune disorder in which antibodies directed against phospholipid-binding proteins drive recurrent arterial or venous thrombosis, microvascular disease, and pregnancy morbidity. It affects approximately 40-50 per 100,000 adults, accounts for roughly 10-15% of all deep vein thromboses and around 20% of strokes in adults under 50, and is the most common acquired cause of recurrent pregnancy loss. Patients typically present with an unexplained clot, repeated miscarriages, transient ischaemic attacks, livedo reticularis, or thrombocytopenia, with persistent antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein-I) on two tests at least 12 weeks apart. Untreated APS carries high recurrence risk and a 5-year mortality near 9% in long-term cohorts. Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin reduces recurrent thrombosis by 80-90% in triple-positive patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 40-50 per 100,000 adults; antiphospholipid antibodies in 1-5% of the general population but most do not develop the syndrome
keyStats.demographics: Women 3-5× more affected than men (mainly because of pregnancy presentations); over 30% of patients also have systemic lupus erythematosus
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of diagnosis 30-40 years; secondary APS more common in younger women with lupus
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1-2 million people worldwide live with APS; 10-15% of all DVTs and ~20% of strokes in adults under 50 are APS-related

definition: Antiphospholipid syndrome (ICD-10: D68.61) is a systemic autoimmune disorder characterized by venous, arterial, or microvascular thrombosis, pregnancy morbidity, or both, in patients with persistent antiphospholipid antibodies. The pathogenic antibodies — lupus anticoagulant (LA), anti-cardiolipin (aCL), and anti-β2-glycoprotein-I (anti-β2GPI) — bind plasma proteins that interact with phospholipids on endothelial cells, platelets, monocytes, and trophoblasts, activating coagulation, complement, and inflammation. The 2023 ACR/EULAR classification criteria (replacing the Sapporo 2006 criteria) require persistent positivity on at least two tests 12 weeks apart together with clinical manifestations weighted by domain (macrovascular venous and arterial thrombosis, microvascular disease, obstetric morbidity, cardiac valve disease, and thrombocytopenia). APS is described as primary when no other autoimmune disease is present, or secondary when it occurs alongside systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis, or other connective tissue disease. Roughly 30-50% of APS patients have associated lupus. Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome (CAPS, also called Asherson syndrome) is a rare, life-threatening variant with rapid multi-organ thrombosis and 30-50% mortality. APS is managed by rheumatologists, haematologists, obstetricians, and stroke physicians in a multidisciplinary fashion, with lifelong anticoagulation in most patients with thrombotic disease.

typesClassification.description: Antiphospholipid syndrome without an underlying autoimmune disease. Accounts for roughly 50% of cases. Patients typically present in their 20s-40s with deep vein thrombosis, stroke, or pregnancy loss.

APS in the context of another autoimmune disease, most commonly systemic lupus erythematosus (in 30-50% of APS cases). Management is the same as primary APS plus disease-specific therapy for the underlying condition.

Defined by recurrent pregnancy loss (≥3 first-trimester miscarriages, or one fetal loss at ≥10 weeks, or premature birth at ≤34 weeks due to severe preeclampsia or placental insufficiency) with antiphospholipid antibody positivity. Combination of low-dose aspirin and prophylactic heparin during pregnancy is the standard.

Persistent positivity for lupus anticoagulant plus anticardiolipin plus anti-β2-glycoprotein-I antibodies in the same patient. Highest risk of thrombosis recurrence; warfarin (not direct oral anticoagulants) is preferred.

Rapid (under 1 week) multi-organ failure from widespread small-vessel thrombosis affecting at least three organs. Mortality 30-50% even with treatment. Requires anticoagulation, high-dose steroids, plasma exchange, intravenous immunoglobulin, and often rituximab or eculizumab.

Clinical features of APS with negative standard antibody tests but positive for non-criteria antibodies (anti-phosphatidylserine/prothrombin, IgA isotypes, anti-domain I of β2GPI). Controversial; management is similar but evidence base is weaker.

primarySymptoms: Deep vein thrombosis of the legs, often unprovoked or recurrent despite standard prophylaxis, presenting with calf swelling, pain, and warmth.
Pulmonary embolism with sudden chest pain, breathlessness, haemoptysis, or syncope.
Ischaemic stroke or transient ischaemic attack in adults under 50 with unilateral weakness, slurred speech, or visual loss.
Recurrent first-trimester miscarriages (three or more) or fetal loss after 10 weeks of pregnancy.
Pre-eclampsia, severe placental insufficiency, intrauterine growth restriction, or premature delivery before 34 weeks.
Livedo reticularis: persistent mottled purplish skin pattern on the legs, arms, or trunk that does not fade with warming.
Mild to moderate thrombocytopenia (platelet count typically 50-140 × 10⁹/L) without significant bleeding.
Cardiac valve thickening or vegetations on echocardiography (Libman-Sacks endocarditis), causing valve regurgitation in some patients.
Migraine or persistent headache, sometimes with cognitive complaints (memory lapses, brain fog).
Skin ulcers, digital ischaemia, splinter haemorrhages, or anetoderma from microvascular thrombosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained deep vein thrombosis, especially in an adult under 50 or with no provoking factor
Two or more first-trimester pregnancy losses, especially with positive lupus anticoagulant on prior testing
Transient ischaemic attack or stroke in a young adult without traditional cardiovascular risk factors
Persistent livedo reticularis that does not fade with warming
Mild persistent thrombocytopenia without infection, bleeding, or chemotherapy exposure
Prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) discovered incidentally on routine bloodwork
emergencySigns: Sudden severe chest pain, breathlessness, or syncope — exclude pulmonary embolism with urgent CT pulmonary angiogram
Sudden focal weakness, slurred speech, or visual loss — activate stroke pathway within 4.5 hours of onset
Multi-organ deterioration (renal failure, encephalopathy, respiratory failure, skin necrosis) within a week — suspect catastrophic APS and admit to critical care
Severe abdominal pain with vomiting and bloody stool — exclude mesenteric ischaemia in known APS
Heavy vaginal bleeding or sudden cessation of fetal movement in pregnancy — emergency obstetric review
Sudden severe limb pain, pallor, and pulselessness — limb ischaemia from arterial thrombosis

causes.description: Pathogenic antibodies recognize β2-glycoprotein-I bound to phospholipid surfaces. They activate endothelial cells, platelets, and monocytes, promote tissue-factor expression, complement activation, and trophoblast injury, producing the prothrombotic and pregnancy-toxic phenotype.

Systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis, and mixed connective tissue disease provide the immunological context in roughly 30-50% of patients. Treatment of the underlying disease alongside anticoagulation reduces flare-related thrombosis risk.

Viral and bacterial infections (HIV, hepatitis C, syphilis, COVID-19) can produce transient antiphospholipid antibodies. These rarely cause sustained syndrome but can trigger transient thrombosis during illness and disappear with recovery.

Chlorpromazine, hydralazine, procainamide, quinidine, and some antibiotics produce antiphospholipid antibodies; these are usually low-titre and non-pathogenic but can occasionally cause syndromal disease.
HLA-DR7 and HLA-DR4 alleles are over-represented in APS, and familial clustering occurs. Genetic susceptibility interacts with environmental triggers in the second-hit hypothesis of APS pathogenesis.

Surgery, immobilisation, oestrogen-containing contraceptives, pregnancy, infection, and trauma can convert asymptomatic antiphospholipid antibody positivity into clinical APS by adding a prothrombotic stress that overwhelms anticoagulant defences.

riskFactors.description: Up to 30-40% of patients with SLE have antiphospholipid antibodies and 15-30% develop APS during follow-up. Lupus patients with triple antibody positivity face the highest thrombotic risk.
Women are diagnosed 3-5× more often than men, partly because of pregnancy presentations and the female predominance of associated autoimmune diseases.
Persistent positivity for lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, and anti-β2-glycoprotein-I confers the highest thrombosis risk, with annual event rates of 5-7% even on anticoagulation.
HLA-DR7 and HLA-DR4 alleles raise susceptibility; first-degree relatives have approximately 2-fold background risk of antiphospholipid antibodies.

riskFactors.factor: Use of oestrogen-containing contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy
riskFactors.description: Combined oral contraceptives raise thrombosis risk 3-5× in healthy women and 10-15× in women with antiphospholipid antibodies. Progestogen-only methods or non-hormonal contraception are recommended in APS.
Physiological hypercoagulability of pregnancy and puerperium markedly increases thrombosis risk in patients with antiphospholipid antibodies; prophylactic heparin is standard during pregnancy.
Any second-hit prothrombotic stress can trigger acute thrombosis in APS. Enhanced perioperative anticoagulation prophylaxis is indicated.
Active smoking magnifies arterial thrombotic risk in APS; cessation is essential for cardiovascular risk reduction.

affectedDemographics: Women of reproductive age (20-45 years) represent the largest demographic, often presenting with pregnancy complications or unprovoked DVT.
Approximately 30-50% of APS patients have systemic lupus erythematosus, mostly young women.
Patients with stroke under 50: APS accounts for 10-20%, especially in women with no traditional cardiovascular risk factors.
Patients with recurrent miscarriage: APS accounts for 10-15% of recurrent first-trimester loss and a higher proportion of second-trimester loss.
Catastrophic APS is rare (~1% of APS cases) but has a 30-50% mortality even with modern treatment.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical features with persistent laboratory evidence of antiphospholipid antibodies. The 2023 ACR/EULAR APS classification criteria require an entry criterion (at least one clinical event and at least one positive antiphospholipid antibody test) plus a weighted score across six domains: macrovascular venous thromboembolism, macrovascular arterial thrombosis, microvascular disease, obstetric morbidity, cardiac valve disease, and haematological. Laboratory testing uses three assays: lupus anticoagulant (LA, performed by dilute Russell viper venom time and activated partial thromboplastin time mixing studies), anti-cardiolipin IgG and IgM (ELISA, with high titre defined as ≥40 GPL or MPL units), and anti-β2-glycoprotein-I IgG and IgM (ELISA, high titre ≥40 units). Any positive test must be repeated after at least 12 weeks to confirm persistence — single positive results are not diagnostic. Patients are stratified as low-, medium-, or high-risk based on antibody profile (single vs triple positivity) and titre. Additional investigations include full blood count for thrombocytopenia, prothrombin time, activated partial thromboplastin time, fibrinogen, D-dimer, renal function, liver function, antinuclear antibody and complement (to screen for lupus), echocardiogram for valve disease, and imaging for current or past thrombosis (Doppler ultrasound for DVT, CTPA for pulmonary embolism, MRI brain for stroke or ischaemic lesions). Genetic thrombophilia testing (factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, antithrombin, protein C and S) is performed in selected cases. Differential diagnosis includes inherited thrombophilia, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria, myeloproliferative disorders, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome, vasculitis, and infective endocarditis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects functional inhibitor of phospholipid-dependent coagulation reactions; most predictive single test for thrombosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample sent to a specialist coagulation laboratory; results in 3-7 days. Must be drawn off therapeutic anticoagulants because heparin and direct oral anticoagulants interfere with the assay.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-cardiolipin antibodies (IgG and IgM by ELISA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies antibody titre against cardiolipin-β2GPI complex
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within 3-7 days. Titres ≥40 GPL or MPL units count as positive in 2023 ACR/EULAR criteria.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-β2-glycoprotein-I antibodies (IgG and IgM by ELISA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antibodies against β2GPI, the principal autoantigen in APS
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as anticardiolipin. Combination of LA, aCL, and anti-β2GPI positivity defines triple-positive APS with highest thrombosis risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms persistence; transient positivity (infection, drugs) is excluded
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Second blood draw at least 12 weeks after first. Both samples must be positive to satisfy classification criteria.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, prothrombin time, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects thrombocytopenia and screens for active coagulation; aPTT may be prolonged because of lupus anticoagulant
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests; results within hours. Mild thrombocytopenia (platelets 50-140 × 10⁹/L) is common.

diagnosticTests.test: Doppler ultrasound of legs, CT pulmonary angiogram, MRI brain
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies acute or past thrombosis informing clinical criteria and treatment intensity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Doppler ultrasound is painless and takes 20-30 minutes; CTPA and MRI take 15-30 minutes. Performed when symptoms suggest vascular events.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for Libman-Sacks endocarditis and valve thickening, present in 30-40% of APS patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan; transoesophageal is performed under light sedation when transthoracic is non-diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies underlying systemic lupus erythematosus or other connective tissue disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw and urine sample. Positive results may indicate secondary APS and trigger additional rheumatological investigation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is tailored to the clinical phenotype: thrombotic, obstetric, asymptomatic high-risk, or catastrophic. For patients with prior venous thrombosis, lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin to a target INR of 2.0-3.0 is the standard. The TRAPS randomized trial (Pengo et al. 2018) found that rivaroxaban was inferior to warfarin in triple-positive APS, with higher rates of arterial thrombosis; current EULAR and ACR guidance therefore restricts direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) to selected patients with single antibody positivity and isolated venous thrombosis, after multidisciplinary discussion. For patients with arterial thrombosis (stroke, myocardial infarction), warfarin to INR 2.0-3.0 with or without low-dose aspirin (75-100 mg/day) is preferred; some centres aim for INR 3.0-4.0 in recurrent arterial events. Patients with persistent triple-positive antibodies but no prior thrombosis (primary thromboprophylaxis) are usually offered low-dose aspirin and aggressive cardiovascular risk reduction; hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg daily is added in lupus-associated APS based on observational data showing reduced thrombosis rates. Obstetric APS management combines low-dose aspirin 75-100 mg daily started before conception with prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg daily or dalteparin 5,000 units daily, doubled to therapeutic in patients with prior thrombosis) from a positive pregnancy test until 6 weeks postpartum; this combination raises live-birth rates from below 30% (no treatment) to 70-80%. Catastrophic APS is treated with full anticoagulation, intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days, plasma exchange or intravenous immunoglobulin, and increasingly with rituximab or eculizumab in refractory cases. Adjunctive measures include strict blood pressure control (<130/80 mmHg), lipid management with statins, smoking cessation, avoidance of oestrogen-containing contraceptives, perioperative bridging with heparin during warfarin interruption, vitamin K avoidance counselling, regular INR monitoring (target ranges 2.0-3.0 or 3.0-4.0 depending on event), and patient education about warning signs of recurrence. Pregnancy planning, contraception, vaccination, and bone health are integral to long-term care.

medicalTreatments.name: Warfarin (target INR 2.0-3.0 for venous events; 3.0-4.0 considered for recurrent arterial events)
medicalTreatments.description: Lifelong oral vitamin K antagonist remains first-line in APS, especially triple-positive patients. Requires regular INR monitoring and careful diet education.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrent venous thrombosis by approximately 80-90% in observational and randomized data

medicalTreatments.name: Low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily or 1.5 mg/kg once daily) for acute thrombosis or pregnancy
medicalTreatments.description: First-line acute anticoagulant for new thrombosis (overlap with warfarin) and for pregnancy prophylaxis throughout gestation and 6 weeks postpartum. Does not cross the placenta.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces miscarriage rates from 70% (untreated) to 20-30% in obstetric APS when combined with low-dose aspirin

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose aspirin 75-100 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Used for primary thromboprophylaxis in asymptomatic high-risk antibody profiles and combined with heparin in obstetric APS. Modest secondary prevention role in arterial events.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces incident thrombosis in primary prophylaxis by approximately 30-50% in observational cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Used in lupus-associated APS and considered in primary APS based on observational data showing reduced thrombosis recurrence. Requires baseline and annual ophthalmological screening.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50-70% reduction in thrombosis recurrence in lupus-APS observational studies; modest benefit on pregnancy outcomes

medicalTreatments.name: Direct oral anticoagulants (rivaroxaban, apixaban) — selected cases only
medicalTreatments.description: Considered only in single-antibody-positive APS with isolated venous thromboembolism and no high-risk features. TRAPS trial showed inferiority to warfarin in triple-positive APS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to warfarin in selected single-positive patients with venous thrombosis; inferior in triple-positive disease

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose corticosteroids plus plasma exchange or IVIG for catastrophic APS
medicalTreatments.description: Methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days followed by oral taper, combined with plasma exchange or intravenous immunoglobulin 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days. Rituximab or eculizumab added in refractory cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduced from 50% (anticoagulation alone) to 25-30% with triple therapy in CAPS registry

medicalTreatments.name: Antihypertensive and statin therapy
medicalTreatments.description: ACE inhibitors or ARBs target blood pressure <130/80 mmHg; statins (atorvastatin 20-40 mg, rosuvastatin 10-20 mg) reduce cardiovascular events and have pleiotropic anti-inflammatory effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Statins reduce cardiovascular events by approximately 25-30% in high-risk patients; tight BP control reduces stroke risk by 30%

surgicalOptions.name: Mechanical thrombectomy for acute stroke
surgicalOptions.description: Endovascular clot retrieval within 24 hours of acute large-vessel ischaemic stroke. Indicated for proximal large-vessel occlusion with salvageable brain on imaging.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional independence at 90 days in 40-50% of treated patients (combined registries)

surgicalOptions.name: Inferior vena cava filter placement
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for patients with acute venous thromboembolism in whom anticoagulation is contraindicated (active major bleeding) or has failed despite therapeutic levels.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces pulmonary embolism recurrence in the short term but does not improve mortality and carries late filter-related complications

surgicalOptions.name: Valve replacement for Libman-Sacks endocarditis
surgicalOptions.description: Performed for severe mitral or aortic regurgitation, refractory heart failure, or recurrent embolic events. Mechanical valves require lifelong anticoagulation to a higher INR.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival 70-85% in published surgical series; rethrombosis risk remains elevated

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasma exchange (therapeutic plasmapheresis)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in catastrophic APS and selected refractory cases to remove pathogenic antibodies and inflammatory mediators. Typically 5-7 sessions; combined with steroids and IVIG.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
alternativeTreatments.description: 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days; alternative or adjunct to plasma exchange in catastrophic APS and used in refractory obstetric APS unresponsive to aspirin and heparin.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist or haematologist with expertise in APS confirms persistent antibody positivity, classifies risk, plans the right anticoagulant strategy, and coordinates obstetric and stroke care. Multidisciplinary follow-up is essential, particularly during pregnancy and around surgery, because incorrect anticoagulation strategy is a leading cause of recurrent thrombosis and obstetric loss.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a centre with a dedicated APS or thrombosis clinic, access to high-quality lupus anticoagulant and anti-β2GPI testing, anticoagulation monitoring service, high-risk obstetric clinic, and an established stroke pathway. Ask about experience with catastrophic APS, pregnancy outcomes, and use of DOACs in selected cases.
hospitalCriteria: Specialist coagulation laboratory performing lupus anticoagulant by dRVVT and aPTT mixing studies
Dedicated APS or thrombosis outpatient clinic
High-risk obstetric clinic for pregnancy management
Stroke unit with mechanical thrombectomy capability
Critical care with plasma exchange and immunomodulation expertise
keyFacilities: Lupus and connective tissue disease clinics
preventionStrategies: Avoid oestrogen-containing contraceptives and hormone replacement therapy in patients with antiphospholipid antibodies.
Use prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin during pregnancy and the postpartum period (6 weeks) in women with antibodies, even without prior thrombosis.
Provide enhanced perioperative thromboprophylaxis around any surgery or prolonged immobilisation.
Stop smoking permanently to reduce arterial thrombosis risk.
Maintain blood pressure under 130/80 mmHg and LDL cholesterol below 2.6 mmol/L (100 mg/dL).
Take influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations to reduce infection-triggered flare risk.
Maintain a healthy weight (BMI 20-25) and engage in regular aerobic exercise.
Wear medical alert identification and carry a steroid/anticoagulant card during travel and emergencies.
lifestyleModifications: Take anticoagulation at the same time daily and maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake (leafy greens) for warfarin users.
Avoid contact sports and high-risk activities that may cause bleeding while on anticoagulation.
Wear compression stockings (class 2) after deep vein thrombosis to reduce post-thrombotic syndrome.
Plan pregnancy with the obstetric and rheumatology team at least 3 months in advance.
Use non-hormonal contraception (intrauterine device, progestogen-only pill, condoms) until planned pregnancy.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol to under 14 units per week.
Hydrate well and walk every 1-2 hours during long flights or car journeys.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruit, vegetables, oily fish, olive oil, and whole grains for cardiovascular protection
Consistent moderate intake of vitamin-K-containing foods (spinach, kale) for stable INR on warfarin
Oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 anti-inflammatory effect
Adequate folate, vitamin B12, and iron during pregnancy
avoid: Sudden large changes in vitamin K intake while on warfarin
Excess alcohol (>14 units/week) which interferes with warfarin metabolism
Grapefruit juice which interacts with several medications
Saturated and trans fats that worsen cardiovascular risk

exerciseGuidelines: Encourage 150 minutes weekly of moderate aerobic activity (walking, swimming, cycling) plus resistance training twice weekly. Avoid contact sports and activities with high bleeding risk. After acute thrombosis, gradual return to activity at 4-6 weeks under medical advice.
dailyManagement: Take warfarin or DOAC at the same time daily and record the dose.
Attend regular INR monitoring (weekly initially, then every 4-6 weeks once stable) for warfarin users.
Carry an anticoagulation card and medical alert identification at all times.
Inspect legs daily for new swelling, pain, or warmth and report promptly.
Monitor blood pressure twice weekly and keep readings below 130/80 mmHg.
Use a pill organiser and set medication reminders to prevent missed doses.
Schedule pregnancy and surgery plans with the rheumatology team well in advance.
prognosis: Outcome depends on antibody profile, end-organ damage, and adherence to anticoagulation. Patients with single-antibody, single-event APS on appropriate warfarin therapy have annual recurrence rates of 1-3% and near-normal life expectancy. Triple-positive patients face higher recurrence (5-7% per year even on warfarin) and worse vascular outcomes; cumulative 10-year survival is 85-90% in modern cohorts. Catastrophic APS still carries 30-50% mortality despite treatment. Quality of life is reduced by chronic anticoagulation, fear of recurrence, cognitive complaints, and pregnancy complications, but most patients lead full lives. Obstetric APS outcomes have transformed: live-birth rates rose from below 30% (no treatment) to 70-80% with aspirin and heparin combination therapy. Long-term morbidity includes post-thrombotic syndrome, chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension, vascular dementia, and accelerated atherosclerosis; aggressive cardiovascular risk reduction is essential.
recoveryTimeline: After acute venous thrombosis, symptoms improve over 2-4 weeks with anticoagulation; post-thrombotic syndrome may persist. Stroke recovery follows standard neurorehabilitation. Pregnancy outcomes are best when treatment starts before conception. Lifelong anticoagulation is the norm; some patients with low-risk profiles may stop anticoagulation under specialist supervision after sustained antibody seronegativity over years.
complications: Recurrent thrombosis (5-10% per year in untreated APS; 1-7% on anticoagulation) — adjust target INR or switch agent.
Catastrophic APS (life-threatening multi-organ thrombosis) — admit to critical care for combined anticoagulation, steroids, plasma exchange/IVIG.
Pregnancy loss, pre-eclampsia, and intrauterine growth restriction — managed by combined low-dose aspirin and prophylactic heparin.
Cardiac valve disease (Libman-Sacks endocarditis) leading to regurgitation, embolism, or heart failure — monitor with serial echocardiography.
Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension after recurrent pulmonary embolism — screen with echocardiography and specialist referral.
Post-thrombotic syndrome with chronic leg swelling, pain, and ulceration — prevent with compression therapy and anticoagulation adherence.
Major bleeding from anticoagulation (1-3% per year) — minimize with INR monitoring, blood pressure control, and avoidance of interacting drugs.

supportResources.name: APS Foundation of America
supportResources.description: Leading US patient advocacy group offering education, helplines, and support communities.

supportResources.name: APS Support UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing support, conferences, and access to specialist APS clinics.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — APS patient information
supportResources.description: Patient-focused overview of APS from the leading US rheumatology professional body.

supportResources.name: EULAR — Antiphospholipid syndrome recommendations
supportResources.description: European recommendations on APS management for clinicians and informed patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus is a multi-system autoimmune disease with positive ANA and anti-dsDNA, often producing arthritis, rash, nephritis, and serositis. APS is defined by thrombosis and pregnancy morbidity with persistent antiphospholipid antibodies; the two coexist in 30-50% of APS cases (secondary APS).

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus Anticoagulant Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus anticoagulant is one of the three antibody tests that supports APS diagnosis. Isolated lupus anticoagulant positivity without thrombotic or obstetric events is not APS but warrants surveillance because it carries the highest single-antibody thrombosis risk.

confusedWithConditions.name: Autoimmune Haemolytic Anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia is associated with APS and lupus and presents with anaemia, raised LDH, and positive direct Coombs test. APS in isolation typically presents with thrombosis or thrombocytopenia rather than haemolytic anaemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke (other causes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: APS-related stroke occurs in younger adults without traditional cardiovascular risk factors and is associated with persistent antiphospholipid antibodies. Non-APS strokes are usually attributable to atherosclerosis, atrial fibrillation, or cardioembolism in older patients.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common autoimmune disease coexisting with APS; lupus patients with antiphospholipid antibodies have the highest thrombosis risk.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: APS causes approximately 20% of strokes in adults under 50; treatment with warfarin reduces recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another autoimmune disease in the same diagnostic family; modestly associated with antiphospholipid antibodies.

relatedConditions.name: Sjögren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Often coexists with lupus and APS in the same patient as part of overlap connective tissue disease.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: APS can cause autoimmune haemolytic anaemia and thrombotic microangiopathy with anaemia; investigation and treatment differ from iron-deficiency anaemia.

faqs.question: What is antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Antiphospholipid syndrome is an autoimmune disease in which antibodies against phospholipid-binding proteins cause recurrent blood clots in veins and arteries, pregnancy complications including recurrent miscarriage, and microvascular disease. Diagnosis requires persistent positive antibody tests on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include unexplained deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke in young adults, recurrent miscarriage, livedo reticularis (mottled purple skin), mild thrombocytopenia, migraine, and heart valve abnormalities. Many patients have no symptoms until a thrombotic event occurs.

faqs.question: How is antiphospholipid syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires at least one clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity) plus persistent positive antiphospholipid antibody testing — lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin IgG/IgM, or anti-β2-glycoprotein-I IgG/IgM — on two separate samples at least 12 weeks apart.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Treatment is lifelong anticoagulation, usually warfarin to an INR of 2.0-3.0, after a first thrombotic event. Obstetric APS combines low-dose aspirin with prophylactic heparin during pregnancy. Hydroxychloroquine is added in lupus-associated APS. Catastrophic APS needs steroids, plasma exchange, and IVIG.

faqs.question: Can antiphospholipid syndrome be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for APS, but lifelong anticoagulation reduces recurrent thrombosis by 80-90% and combined aspirin-heparin therapy raises live-birth rates from below 30% to 70-80% in obstetric APS. Some patients with persistent antibody seronegativity may stop anticoagulation under specialist supervision.

faqs.question: Is APS the same as lupus?
faqs.answer: APS and lupus are related but distinct autoimmune diseases. About 30-50% of APS patients also have lupus (secondary APS); the rest have primary APS without other autoimmune disease. Both are managed by rheumatologists, and lupus-APS patients require treatment for both conditions.

faqs.question: Can I have a baby with antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. With pre-pregnancy planning, low-dose aspirin from before conception, and prophylactic heparin throughout pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum, live-birth rates reach 70-80%. Pregnancy should be managed at a high-risk obstetric clinic with rheumatology and haematology input.

faqs.question: Can I take direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) for APS?
faqs.answer: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) are not recommended for triple-positive APS because the TRAPS trial showed higher arterial thrombosis with rivaroxaban than warfarin. DOACs may be considered in single-antibody-positive patients with isolated venous thromboembolism after specialist multidisciplinary discussion.

faqs.question: What is catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Catastrophic APS (CAPS) is a rare, life-threatening variant with widespread small-vessel thrombosis in at least three organs developing within a week. Treatment combines anticoagulation, high-dose steroids, plasma exchange, intravenous immunoglobulin, and increasingly rituximab or eculizumab. Mortality is 30-50% despite treatment.

faqs.question: What is the lupus anticoagulant test?
faqs.answer: The lupus anticoagulant test detects a functional antibody that prolongs phospholipid-dependent clotting assays in vitro despite promoting thrombosis in vivo. It is performed by dilute Russell viper venom time and activated partial thromboplastin time mixing studies. A positive test must be confirmed after 12 weeks.

faqs.question: Does APS cause headaches and brain fog?
faqs.answer: Many APS patients report migraine, persistent headache, and cognitive difficulties (memory lapses, brain fog) without overt stroke. Microvascular brain injury detectable on MRI may contribute. Anticoagulation, hydroxychloroquine, and migraine prophylaxis may help; significant cognitive decline warrants neurological assessment.

faqs.question: Can APS go into remission?
faqs.answer: APS is a chronic condition without true remission, but patients can become persistently antibody-seronegative over years. Discontinuing anticoagulation in such patients is occasionally considered under specialist supervision, with close monitoring for recurrence. Most patients remain on lifelong anticoagulation.

faqs.question: Can children get antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Paediatric APS is rare but well described, with similar antibody profiles and thrombotic manifestations as adult disease. It often coexists with paediatric lupus. Management is similar but requires careful dosing of warfarin or LMWH and consideration of growth and development.

faqs.question: What lifestyle changes help with APS?
faqs.answer: Stop smoking, avoid oestrogen-containing contraceptives, maintain a healthy weight, follow a Mediterranean-style diet, exercise regularly, control blood pressure and cholesterol, and stay well hydrated. Wear compression stockings after DVT and walk frequently during long travel.

faqs.question: How often do I need INR monitoring on warfarin?
faqs.answer: INR is checked weekly when starting warfarin until stable in the target range, then every 4-6 weeks. More frequent testing is needed during illness, after antibiotic courses, dietary change, or other medication adjustments. Self-testing devices are useful for selected motivated patients.

faqs.question: Is APS hereditary?
faqs.answer: APS is not directly inherited but has a modest genetic predisposition through HLA-DR4 and HLA-DR7 alleles. First-degree relatives have approximately 2-fold higher background risk of antiphospholipid antibodies, but most do not develop clinical APS. Routine family screening is not recommended.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with antiphospholipid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Moderate aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, cycling) at least 150 minutes per week is encouraged for cardiovascular protection. Avoid contact sports and high-risk activities on anticoagulation. After acute thrombosis, return to activity gradually over 4-6 weeks under medical advice.

faqs.question: What are the triggers for APS thrombosis?
faqs.answer: Triggers include surgery, trauma, prolonged immobilisation, infection, pregnancy, oestrogen contraceptives, smoking, dehydration, and discontinuation of anticoagulation. Enhanced thromboprophylaxis is needed around these events. Patients should avoid oestrogen contraception and inform clinicians of their diagnosis before any surgery.

faqs.question: Are vaccinations safe in APS?
faqs.answer: Yes. Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations are recommended to reduce infection-triggered flares. COVID-19 vaccination is safe and recommended. Live vaccines are generally avoided in patients on high-dose immunosuppression; discuss timing with the rheumatology team.

faqs.question: What is Hughes syndrome?
faqs.answer: Hughes syndrome is another name for antiphospholipid syndrome, after Dr Graham Hughes who described the condition in 1983. The terms are interchangeable. Some patient organisations and UK clinicians use Hughes syndrome to highlight the clinical features beyond purely laboratory abnormalities.

faqs.question: Can APS cause heart problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. APS can cause Libman-Sacks endocarditis (thickened valves and vegetations), myocardial infarction, cardiomyopathy, and accelerated atherosclerosis. Echocardiography is recommended at diagnosis and periodically thereafter, especially in patients with stroke or systemic embolism.

metaDescription: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS, Hughes): clots, miscarriage, lupus anticoagulant, warfarin therapy, pregnancy care. Medically reviewed expert guide.
